content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 25, 2017

Michael Torres
Assistant City Attorney
City of Newport Beach
100 Civic Center Drive
Newport Beach, CA 92660

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-233

Dear Mr. Torres:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Moreover, we base this letter on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

Can a council member with substantial business activity related to the use of the Newport Harbor, including two long term leases for property adjacent to the harbor and the exclusive use of a marina under a commercial pier permit, take part in decisions to redraft the City's code regarding the management and use of the harbor?

CONCLUSION

The councilmember may not take part in the decisions. Considering the councilmember's substantial business interests in the harbor, the councilmember's business is the subject of a proceeding to redraft the City's code as it pertains to the management and use of the harbor, and the financial effect of the decision on the business is both foreseeable and material.

FACTS

Councilmember Duffield was elected to the Newport Beach City Council at the November 4, 2014, General Election. For the past 45 years, Councilmember Duffield has been

___________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-233
Page No. 2

building boats in California and is the owner and Chief Executive Officer of Duffield Marine
Incorporated ("Duffy Boats"). Duffy Boats is the leader in the leisure electric boat market, having
built over 13,000 electric boats. Over 3,500 electric boats constructed and sold by Duffy Boats are
in service in Newport Harbor.

Duffy Boats leases two properties in the City under long term leases adjacent to Newport
Harbor including a boat rental and sales facility and a boat service facility. Councilmember Duffield
also owns a personal residence located approximately 560 feet from Newport Harbor. In addition to
the leased property, Duffy Boats has an annual commercial pier permit from the City for the boat
rental and sales facility. This permit allows Duffy Boats to maintain a marina over publicly owned
tidelands in Newport Harbor. Duffy Boats' marina is used exclusively by the company and is not
subleased to third parties.

Duffy Boats' primary business focuses on sales, rentals and service of electric boats. Duffy
Boats also provides a monthly maintenance plan to electric boat owners called "Duffy Care." For
purposes of this request, you ask that we assume that Duffy Boats may receive over $500 from a
client for an individual sale, rental, service, or annual Duffy Care plan.

Councilmember Duffield's job duties are varied. He plays an active role in creating new
products for his company and driving the overall direction of the company. As the owner and CEO,
Councilmember Duffield controls, directs, and manages all aspects of Duffy Boats' operations.
Duffy Boats' annual revenue, on a company-wide basis is over $2.5 million. As the sole owner,
Councilmember Duffield owns 100 percent of Duffy Boats' privately held shares. For purposes of
this request, you ask that we assume these shares exceed $2,000 in value.

The City, via legislative tidelands grants, has oversight and management of tidelands, which
includes, but is not limited to, the land located beneath Newport Harbor. Under this authority, the
City developed Newport Beach Municipal Code Title 17. According to Subsection 17.25.020(A),
the purpose of Title 17 includes, but is not limited to, preservation of "the diverse uses of the harbor
and the waterfront that contribute to the charm and character of Newport Bay, and [to] provide
needed support for recreational boaters, visitors, and residents with regulations limited to those
necessary to protect the interests of all users." Title 17 regulates all aspects of Newport Harbor
including, but not limited to, vessel operation and storage, commercial and recreational uses,
mooring administration, harbor usage, and dredging.

The City anticipates conducting a major redrafting of Title 17 soon to ensure
consistency and compatibility with recent changes in the City's management of Newport Harbor.
Newport Harbor is one of the largest recreational harbors in the United States and houses thousands
of vessels on moorings, anchorages, slips, and docks. Thousands of residences and commercial
operations border the waters of Newport Harbor.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
(Section 87103.) A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. Different standards

File No. A-17-233
Page No. 3

apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest.

Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103. Those interests implicated by your account of the facts are the following:

• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)).

• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b)) including a pro rata share of interests in real property of any business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater (Section 82033).

• An interest in a source of income to the official, or promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c)) including a pro rata share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official (or his or her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent or greater interest (Section 82030(a)).

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.)²

Based upon the facts provided, Councilmember Duffield has interests in Duffy Boats as both a business entity and source of income. Councilmember Duffy also has multiple interests in real property including his residence and, as the sole owner of Duffy Boats, both of Duffy Boats' leases adjacent to the harbor.

Foreseeability: Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 1 8702.2(a)(1)-(6). For any interest "not explicitly involved" in the decisions, a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).)

Materiality: A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. (Regulation 18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest.

________________________
² While an official always has an interest in his or her personal finances, including those of an immediate family member (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(c)(6)), an effect on an official's personal finances is not considered separately where the decision affects the official's interest in a business entity or real property (Regulation 18702.5(c)). Accordingly, the personal financial effects rule will not be discussed further.

File No. A-17-233
Page No. 4

Under Regulation 18702.1(a)(6), a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest in a business entity is the subject of a "proceeding subject to the regulatory authority of the official's agency." Under the facts provided, Title 17 regulates all aspects of Newport Harbor including vessel operation and storage, commercial and recreational uses, mooring administration, harbor development, harbor usage, and dredging. Considering its substantial business interest in the harbor, Duffy Boats is the subject of a proceeding to redraft Title 17, and the financial effect of the decision on Duffy Boats is both foreseeable and material. Thus, Councilmember Duffield may not take part in the decisions.³

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel,
Legal Division

BGL:jgl

³ In light of the conclusion that Councilmember Duffield is disqualified based upon his interest in Duffy Boats as a business entity and source of income, it is unnecessary to further consider his interest in his residence or Duffy Boats' leases adjacent to the harbor.

Can a council member with substantial business activity related to the use of the Newport 
Harbor, including two long term leases for property adjacent to the harbor and the exclusive use of 
a marina under a commercial pier permit, take part in decisions to redraft the City's code regarding 
the management and use of the harbor?

The councilmember may not take part in the decisions. Considering the councilmember's 
substantial business interests in the harbor, the councilmember's business is the subject of a 
proceeding to redraft the City's code as it pertains to the management and use of the harbor, and the 
financial effect of the decision on the business is both foreseeable and material.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
(Section 87103.) A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. Different standards  
apply to detennine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be material 
depending on the nature of the interest. 
Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103. Those 
interests implicated by your account of the facts are the following: 
• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)). 
• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or 
more (Section 87103(b)) including a pro rata share of interests in real property of any 
business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, 
indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater (Section 82033 ). 
• An interest in a source of income to the official, or promised income, which aggregates to 
$500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c)) including a pro rata 
share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official ( or his or her spouse) 
owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent or greater interest (Section 82030(a)). 
• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.)2 
Based upon the facts provided, Councilmember Duffield has interests in Duffy Boats as 
both a business entity and source of income. Councilmember Duffy also has multiple interests in 
real property including his residence and, as the sole owner of Duffy Boats, both of Duffy Boats' 
leases adjacent to the harbor. 
Foreseeability: Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the 
interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the 
official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the 
decision involves thf issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, pennit, or 
other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision 
affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation l 8702.2{a)(l )-(6). For any 
interest '"not explicitly involved" in the decisions, a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if the 
effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. A 
financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. {Regulation 18701{b).) 
Materiality: A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of 
a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. (Regulation 18700(a).) Different 
standards apply to detennine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be 
material depending on the nature of the interest. 
2 While an official always has an interest in his or her personal finances, including those of an immediate 
family member (Section 8 7103; Regulation 18700( c )(6)), an effect on an official's personal finances is not considered 
separately where the decision affects the official's interest in a business entity or real property (Regulation 18702.S(c)). 
Accordingly, the personal financial effects rule will not be discussed further.  
Under Regulation 18702.1 (a)(6), a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest in a 
business entity is material anytime the business entity is the subject of a "proceeding subject to the 
regulatory authority of the official's agency." Under the facts provided, Title 17 regulates all 
aspects of Newport Harbor including vessel operation and storage, commercial and recreational 
uses, mooring administration, harbor development, harbor usage, and dredging. Considering its 
substantial business interest in the harbor, Duffy Boats is the subject of a proceeding to redraft Title 
17, and the financial effect of the decision on Duffy Boats is both foreseeable and material. Thus, 
Councilmember Duffield may not take part in the decisions. 3

Harbor, including two long term leases for property adjacent to the harbor and the exclusive use of 
a marina under a commercial pier permit, take part in decisions to redraft the City's code regarding 
the management and use of the harbor?

CONCLUSION: The councilmember may not take part in the decisions. Considering the councilmember's 
substantial business interests in the harbor, the councilmember's business is the subject of a 
proceeding to redraft the City's code as it pertains to the management and use of the harbor, and the 
financial effect of the decision on the business is both foreseeable and material.
